name: M.S. in Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The degree offers comprehensive IS coverage across databases (CS 631/632/731, IS 631), analytics and ML (CS 634/636/675/732, DS 650), networking (CS 651/652/656), security (CS 608/645/646/647/696/708/755), software engineering (CS 673/683/684/685), and web (IS 601), with real graduate depth (deepestPrereqChain=3). But requirementGroups=0 and 80 referenced courses mean there is no visible required core or concentration, and business/HCI/healthcare electives (ACCT 615, IE 661-688) add breadth without a defined path.
dimensions.suggestion: Define a required IS core (data management, systems analysis, analytics) plus named concentrations so the wide elective pool has a coherent progression.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph shows healthy graduate depth: deepestPrereqChain=3 (e.g., CS 608/CS 645/CS 696->CS 708; CS 645->CS 647) with missingCourseCount=0 and no dangling references.
dimensions.suggestion: Maintain the depth while cleaning prerequisite fields where narrative description text has bled in (CS 696, IS 631) so requirements read cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes are present in the data, so the competencies an IS graduate should demonstrate are unstated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 measurable outcomes covering data management, systems analysis/design, and applied analytics.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: For a full M.S., statedTotalCredits is null and requirementGroups=0, so the total (typically ~30 credits) and the core/elective split are not transparent; creditReconcileDelta is null so internal consistency cannot be verified.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and the division among required core, concentration, and free electives.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Course descriptions are strong and specific (DS 650 on human-centered visualization; CS 732 on high-dimensional learning), but the page lacks a plan of study, term labeling, and a credit total, and the 80-course undifferentiated list gives no sense of how a student completes the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the entry with a labeled plan of study, credit summary, and a curated core-versus-concentration course organization.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The electives are current and employable: machine learning (CS 675/732), big data (CS 644), data visualization (DS 650), R analytics (CS 636), cloud (CS 652), information retrieval (IS 634), and modern security (CS 645/647/708). The gaps are data engineering, MLOps, and generative-AI/LLM content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add data-engineering and MLOps electives and a generative-AI/LLM course to match current data-professional workflows.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are detailed and specific (IS 634 on crawling, indexing, and vector-space retrieval; CS 636 on R-based analytics; CS 708 on data security/privacy paradigms); none are thin or placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are consistent with no dangling references, but many courses list none and several embed description text in the prerequisite field (CS 696, IS 631, IS 601), so clarity varies course to course.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 644 introduces big data, but there is no course on ETL/ELT, warehousing, and streaming pipelines that IS professionals build and operate.
CS 675/732 and CS 634 cover modeling but not productionizing, monitoring, or serving models within information systems.
Cognitive computing (CS 676) is the closest touchpoint; there is no dedicated treatment of LLMs, embeddings, or RAG now central to IS practice.

missingTopics.topic: IS strategy, governance, and digital transformation
missingTopics.rationale: IS 677 introduces IS principles, but strategic alignment, IT governance, and transformation management are not deepened.
CS 708 covers technical data security, but organizational data governance and regulatory compliance are absent.

emergingTrends.rationale: Building on CS 675/CS 676, an applied LLM course would target the fastest-growing IS skill demand.

emergingTrends.trend: Modern data stack and real-time analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: Extending CS 644 and DS 650 toward streaming and self-service BI reflects how organizations now consume data.
As IS embeds ML in decisions, fairness, explainability, and auditability become essential competencies.
CS 652 (cognitive cloud) provides a base to teach lakehouse/warehouse platforms underpinning modern IS.
Coupling IS 661 UX with ML courses addresses the design of AI-augmented workflows.

topSuggestions: Publish a required IS core plus named concentrations and the total credit requirement (~30) to make the 80-course pool navigable.
Add data-engineering and MLOps electives to cover the deploy/operate side of the data lifecycle.
Add a generative-AI/LLM course to close the most significant currency gap.
Publish 3-5 program-level learning outcomes for data management, analysis/design, and analytics.
Rebuild the catalog page with a labeled plan of study and credit summary.
